coredata.dc:title: A novel active equalizer for Li-ion battery pack in electric vehicles
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2019 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yu
Xia, Chaoying
Gu, Miao
Xin, Wei
Men, Xuming
coredata.dc:description: A novel active equalizer for Li-ion battery pack in electric vehicles is designed. Based on cell-to-pack-to-cell topology, the equalizer consists of a switch array and a single-ended forward bidirectional DC-DC converter, which is simple, efficient and reliable. During discharging process, energy flows from the most charged cell to the battery pack, realizing peak-cut equalization. During charging process, energy transfers from the whole battery pack to the least charged cell, enabling valley-filled equalization. Using synchronous rectification, the equalizer therefore has less conduction loss. Finally, a battery management system (BMS) experimental platform for a 6-series Li-ion battery pack is set up. The experimental results show that it can quickly reduce the difference between maximum and minimum SOC value to less than 5%, which prove the feasibility and effectiveness of the designed equalizer.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Li-ion battery
active equalizer
bidirectional DC-DC converter
battery management system

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2019 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.

xocs:doc.xocs:rawtext: ScienceDirect Available online at www.sciencedirect.comAvailable online at w.sciencedirect.com ScienceDirect Energy Procedia 00 (2017) 000â€“000 www.elsevier.com/locate/procedia 1876-6102 Â© 2017 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. Peer-review under responsibility of the Scientific Committee of The 15th International Symposium on District Heating and Cooling. The 15th International Symposium on District Heating and Cooling Assessing the feasibility of using the heat demand-outdoor temperature function for a long-term district heat demand forecast I. AndriÄ‡a,b,c*, A. Pinaa, P. FerrÃ£oa, J. Fournierb., B. LacarriÃ¨rec, O. Le Correc aIN+ Center for Innovation, Technology and Policy Research - Instituto Superior TÃ©cnico, Av. Rovisco Pais 1, 1049-001 Lisbon, Portugal bVeolia Recherche & Innovation, 291 Avenue Dreyfous Daniel, 78520 Limay, France cDÃ©partement SystÃ¨mes Ã‰nergÃ©tiques et Environnement - IMT Atlantique, 4 rue Alfred Kastler, 44300 Nantes, France Abstract District heating networks are commonly addressed in the literature as one of the most effective solutions for decreasing the greenhouse gas emissions from the building sector. These systems require high investments which are returned through the heat sales. Due to the changed climate conditions and building renovation policies, heat demand in the future could decrease, prolonging the investment return period. The main scope of this paper is to assess the feasibility of using the heat demand â€“ outdoor temperature function for heat demand forecast. The district of Alvalade, located in Lisbon (Portugal), was used as a case study. The district is consisted of 665 buildings that vary in both construction period and typology. Three weather scenarios (low, medium, high) and three district renovation scenarios were developed (shallow, intermediate, deep). To estimate the error, obtained heat demand values were compared with results from a dynamic heat demand model, previously developed and validated by the authors. The results showed that when only weather change is considered, the margin of error could be acceptable for some applications (the error in annual demand was lower than 20% for all weather scenarios considered). However, after introducing renovation scenarios, the error value increased up to 59.5% (depending on the weather and renovation scenarios combination considered). The value of slope coefficient increased on average within the range of 3.8% up to 8% per decade, that corresponds to the decrease in the number of heating hours of 22-139h during the heating season (depending on the combination of weather and renovation scenarios considered). On the other hand, function intercept increased for 7.8-12.7% per decade (depending on the coupled scenarios). The values suggested could be used to modify the function parameters for the scenarios considered, and improve the accuracy of heat demand estimations. Â© 2017 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. Peer-review under responsibility of the Scientific Committee of The 15th International Symposium on District Heating and Cooling. Keywords: Heat demand; Forecast; Climate change Energy Procedia 158 (2019) 2649 2654 1876-6102 Â© 2019 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/) Peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of ICAE2018 â€“ The 10th International Conference on Applied Energy. 10.1016/j.egypro.2019.02.017 10.1016/j.egypro.2019.02.017 Â© 2019 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/lice ses/by-nc-nd/4.0/) Peer-review under r ponsibility of the scientific committee of ICAE2018 â€“ The 10th International Conference on Applied Energy. 1876-6102 Available online at www.sciencedirect.com ScienceDirect Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 www.elsevier.com/locate/procedia 1876-6102 Copyright Â© 2018 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Selection and peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the 10th International Conference on Applied Energy (ICAE2018). 10th International Conference on Applied Energy (ICAE2018), 22-25 August 2018, Hong Kong, China A novel active equalizer for Li-ion battery pack in electric vehicles Yu Liu*, Chaoying Xia, Miao Gu, Wei Xin, Xuming Men The school of electrical and information engineering, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300072, China Abstract A novel active equalizer for Li-ion battery pack in electric vehicles is designed. Based on cell-to-pack-to-cell topology, the equalizer consists of a switch array and a single-ended forward bidirectional DC-DC converter, which is simple, efficient and reliable. During discharging process, energy flows from the most charged cell to the battery pack, realizing peak-cut equalization. During charging process, energy transfers from the whole battery pack to the least charged cell, enabling valley-filled equalization. Using synchronous rectification, the equalizer therefore has less conduction loss. Finally, a battery management system (BMS) experimental platform for a 6-series Li-ion battery pack is set up. The experimental results show that it can quickly reduce the difference between maximum and minimum SOC value to less than 5%, which prove the feasibility and effectiveness of the designed equalizer. Copyright Â© 2018 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Selection and peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the 10th International Conference on Applied Energy (ICAE2018). Keywords: Li-ion battery; active equalizer; bidirectional DC-DC converter; battery management system 1. Introduction With the dvantages of high energy density, long cycle life and l w s lf-discharge rate, the Li-io battery is widely used in Electric Vehicl s (EVs). A Li-ion battery pack usually contains hundreds f battery cells i series and parallel, to fulfill the power demand of the EV. Every multi-cell battery pack shoul be equipped with a battery management system (BMS) to ensure the safety and reliability of the battery. Since battery cells have different capacity, impedance and self-discharge rate caused by manufacturing processes, leading to inconformity issues, battery equalization is one of the key function in a BMS. A good equalizer is able to help the BMS prevent over- charge and over-discharge of the battery pack, so as to prolong the battery life. Although it is impossible to achieve * Corresponding author E-mail address: lyonlyou@163.com Available online at www.sciencedirect.com ScienceDirect Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 www.elsevier.com/locate/procedia 1876-6102 Co yright Â© 2018 Els vier Ltd. All rig ts reserved. Selection and peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the 10th International Conference on Applied Energy (ICAE2018). 10th International Conference on Applied Energy (ICAE2018), 22-25 August 2018, Hong Kong, China A novel active equalizer for Li-ion battery pack in electric vehicles Yu Liu*, Chaoying Xia, Miao Gu, Wei Xin, Xuming Men The school of electrical and information engineering, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300072, China Abstract A novel active equalizer for Li-ion battery pack in lect ic vehicles is designed. Based on cell-to-pack-to-ce l topology, the equ lizer consists of switch array a d a single-ended forward bidirectional DC-DC converter, which is simple, fficient and reliable. Du ing discharging process, e ergy flows from the most cha ged cell t the battery pack, realizing pe k-cut equalization. During charging process, energy transfers from the whole battery pack to the least charged cell enabling v lley-filled equalization. Using synchronous rectification, the equal zer ther fore has le s conduction loss. Fi al y, a battery man gement system (BMS) exper m ntal platform for a 6-series Li-ion battery pack is set up. T e experimental results show that it can quickly reduce t ifference between maximum and minimum SOC value to less than 5%, which prove the feasibility and effectiveness of the designed equalizer. Copyr ght Â© 2018 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Selection and peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the 10th International Conference on Applied Energy (ICAE2018). Keywords: Li-ion battery; active equalizer; bidirectional DC-DC converter; battery management system 1. Introduction With the advantages of high energy density, long c cle life nd low self-discharge ate, the Li-i battery is wi ely s d in Ele tric V hicles (EVs). A Li-ion batt r pack usually contains hund eds of battery c lls in series and p rall l, to f lfill the power demand of the EV. Every multi-cell battery ack sh uld be equipped with a batt ry management system (BMS) to ensure the safety and reliability of the battery. Since battery cells have differ nt c pacity, impedance and self-discharge rate caused by manufacturing processes, leading to inconformity issu s, battery equalization is one of the key function in a BMS. A ood equalizer is able to help the BMS prevent over- charge and over-discharge of the battery pack, so as to prolong the battery life. Although it is impossible to achieve * Corresponding author E-mail address: lyonlyou@163.com 2650 Yu Liu et al. / Energy Procedia 158 (2019) 2649â€“26542 Miao Gu et.al / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 absolute consistency, we can keep the equalization index (such as battery cell voltage, capacity, or SOC) into a limited range. According to the characteristics of energy flow, common equalizer topologies can be divided into five categories [1], as shown in Fig. 1. (1) cell bypass [2-3]: Certain cells can be bypassed by their corresponding switches, to prevent them from over- charging. This method cannot deal with over-discharge problem. The conduction loss is quite high, leading to low efficiency and reliability. (2) cell-to-heat [2-3]: Excess energy is transferred to thermal energy, released from the paralleled resistor. It is also called passive equalization, while topologies (3)-(5) belong to active equalization. The equalizing current is relatively small, usually less than a few hundred mA. This method is simple but energy-consuming, heat-dissipating and time-consuming, so it is no longer the focus of recent research. (3) cell-to-cell distributed [2-4]: The extra energy stored in the more charged cell is transferred to the adjacent less charged one. It is a simple and modularized topology, but the energy has to be transmitted for multiple times to achieve final equilibrium. It happens that some cells are unnecessarily charged and discharged for many times, which decreases the speed of equalization and shortens battery life. (4) cell-to-cell shared [2-3] [5]: The most charged cell releases its energy to the least charged cell through one or more energy storage components. This method realizes peak-cut and valley-filled equalization, and overcomes the shortcoming of topology (3) that only can transfer the energy between two neighboring cells. However, its hardware design is complex and expensive. (5) cell-to-pack-to-cell [2-5] [6-8]: The equalizing energy not only flows from the most charged cell to the battery pack to prevent over-charging, but also flows from the battery pack to the least charged cell to avoid over- discharging. This method has advantages of high efficiency, low complexity and bi-directional energy flow. ï¼‹ _ ï¼‹ _ ï¼‹ _ Storage element ï¼‹ _ ï¼‹ _ (1) cell bypass (2) cell-to-heat (3) cell-to-cell distributed (4) cell-to-cell shared (5) cell-to-pack-to-cell Fig. 1. Common topologies of battery equalizers This paper designs an active equalizer for a Li-ion battery pack in electric vehicles based on topology (5). This equalizer maximizes the balancing efficiency and has good application value. To prove its working principle, a BMS experimental platform is built. The experimental results prove the feasibility and effectiveness of the equalizer. 2. Equalizer design 2.1. Main circuit Main circuit of the equalizer is shown in Fig. 2. The high-voltage side is connected to the battery pack, and low- voltage side is connected to a battery cell, which is selected through a switch array and connected to a single-ended forward bidirectional DC-DC converter. The switch array is composed of cell switches S1~S(N+1) and polarity switches P1 and P2. Assumed that the cell to be equalized is numbered k, Sk and S(k+1) should be turned on. If k is an odd number, P1 should connect the lower end and P2 should connect the upper end, while if k is even, P1 should connect the upper end and P2 should connect the lower end. The single-ended forward bidirectional converter contains a transformer, three MOSFETs, an energy storage inductor, and two filter capacitors. Considering forward transformer cores work in the first quadrant of the magnetization curve, to prevent core saturation, the magnetic flux of the transformer must be reduced to zero before Yu Liu et al. / Energy Procedia 158 (2019) 2649â€“2654 2651 Miao Gu et.al / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 3 the end of each switching period. Therefore, we need to add two magnetic reset circuits in both primary and secondary sides of the transformer. In primary side, the magnetic reset circuit is composed of winding Nm and diode D0. In secondary side, the magnetic reset circuit is an RCD circuit, which contains a resistor, a capacitor, and a diode. Cell1 Cell2 Cell3 CellN ï¼‹ S1 S2 S3 S4 SN S(N+1) P1 P2 M2 M3 D0NmNs L Boost Buck Switch array Single-ended forward bidirectional DC-DC converter ... ï¼‹ ï¼� M1 RCD ï¼� Np Ucell Upack Fig. 2. Main circuit 2.2. The advantages of equalizer The designed equalizer has following advantages: Firstly, it uses a switch array to choose the cell to be equalized instead of a multi-winding transistor in [8], avoiding electromagnetic interference problems caused by multiple secondary windings. Secondly, compared with using 2(N+1) switches in [7], this design decreases the number of switches by nearly half to form the switch array, which reduces the control complexity greatly. Thirdly, compared with full-bridge bidirectional converters in [6], single-ended forward bidirectional converters only need three MOSFETs, so the equalizer has a smaller size and lower cost. Fourthly, when using push-pull-forward half-bridge converters in [7], users must adjust the center tap of the transformer in order to realize bidirectional voltage regulation, which is inconvenient and unpractical, while single-ended forward bidirectional converters overcome this problem. In addition, forward transformers have less leakage inductance than flyback transformers in [8] (an air gap should be added to the core of the flyback transformer, which leads to large leakage inductance), the design has less energy loss and higher efficiency, and its output voltage is more stable with less ripple. 3. Working principle 3.1. Discharge equalization During discharge equalization, the switch array selects the cell with the lowest SOC, and the single-ended forward bidirectional converter operates in Buck mode. As shown in Fig. 3(1), M1 is the main switch, whose driving signal is same as M3 and complementary to M2. M2 realizes synchronous rectification. Synchronous rectification means the current flows through a turned-on MOSFET instead of a diode as the on-resistance of a MOSFET is very small, which is a good method to reduce conduction loss and improve system efficiency. Moreover, in case of M2 and M3 are turned on simultaneously, resulting in short-circuiting of the winding Ns, a dead zone is added in M2 and M3 complementary driving signals. [ 0t , 1t ] stage (forward energy flow): M1 and M3 are turned on, M2 is turned off. Energy of the battery pack is transferred to the load through the transformer, and partially stored in inductor L. Magnetic flux of the transformer increases linearly from zero until time t1 when magnetic flux reaches its peak. [ 1t , 2t ] stage (dead zone): M1, M2 and M3 are all off. The current of secondary winding Ns converts to flow through the body diode of M3, then reduces to zero before time t2. Inductor current IL converts to flow from the M3 branch to the M2â€™s body diode branch. Magnetic reset circuit in primary side starts to work, and the excitation energy is fed back to the input through diode D0. Magnetic flux decreases linearly to zero, namely the magnetic flux reset has completed. 2652 Yu Liu et al. / Energy Procedia 158 (2019) 2649â€“26544 Miao Gu et.al / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 [ 2t , 3t ] stage (diode freewheeling): M2 is turned on, M1 and M3 are turned off. Inductor L releases energy to the load through M2. [ 3t , 4t ] stage (dead zone): M1, M2 and M3 are all off, and IL flows through M2â€™s body diode. 3.2. Charge equalization During charge equalization, the switch array selects the cell with the highest SOC, and the single-ended forward bidirectional converter works in Boost mode. As shown in Fig. 3(2), M2 is the main switch, whose driving signal is complementary to M3 and M1. M1 realizes the function of synchronous rectification. [ 0t , 1t ] stage (inductor energy storage): M2 is turned on, M1 and M3 are turned off. Inductor current IL begins to increase linearly. Inductor L stores the energy as magnetic energy. Excitation energy stored in winding Ns is consumed by the resistor R in RCD circuit. [ 1t , 2t ] stage (reverse energy flow): M2 is turned off, M1 and M3 are turned on. IL is commutated from the M2 branch to the M3 branch. Inductor L converts magnetic energy into electrical energy and transfers it to the load. 1t 2t 3t 4t0t t M2 t t M1ã€�M3 t t UNp IL Upack ï¼�Upack Nm /Np Î¦ 1t 2t0t t M2 t M1ã€�M3 t t UL IL Ucell ï¼�Upack /(Np /Nm)ï¼‹Ucell (1) Buck mode (2) Boost mode Fig.3. Working waveforms 3.3. Main parameters Assuming that all devices in the equalizer are ideal components, the entire circuit has no loss and the dead zone of switch driving signals are ignored, according to inductor volt-second balance, the output voltage expressions can be deduced: 1 1 1 (Buck mode) packU U Dn (1) 2 2 (Boost mode) 1 cell nU U D (2) Among them, n denotes the turn ratio of the transformer, 1D denotes the duty cycle of main switch M1 in Buck mode, and 2D denotes the duty cycle of main switch M2 in Boost mode. In order to meet the output voltage requirement, 1D is set to 10.3 0.45Dï‚£ ï‚£ (output voltage > cell voltage), and 2D is set to 20.7 0.9Dï‚£ ï‚£ (output voltage > pack voltage). In order to let the forward bidirectional converter works in CCM (Current Continual Mode), the following two inequalities need to be met simultaneously: 1 1 1( ) (1 )pack cell cellD U U D Un (3) 2 2 1(1 )( )cell pack cellD U D U Un (4) According to (3) and (4), inequality (5) is obtained, based on which, the transformer turn ratio n can be determined. Yu Liu et al. / Energy Procedia 158 (2019) 2649â€“2654 2653 Miao Gu et.al / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 5 2 1(1 ) pack pack cell cell D U DU n U U (5) 4. Experimental verification 4.1. Hardware design When designing the equalizerâ€™s hardware, we chose the IRF540N MOSFET as switch M1, and chose two IRF3205 MOSFETs as switch M2 and M3. We used high-speed MOSFET driver chip TC4420COA in drive circuit. Besides, the working frequency is 100kHz, inductance L is 68uH, and the transformer turns ratio is Nm:Np:Ns = 2:2:1. As shown in Fig. 4, we used N+1 pairs of anti-series MOSFETs as S1~S(N+1), used 4 MOSFETs as P1 and P2. We also applied a switch matrix gate driver chip EMB1428 to drive these MOSFETs. A BMS experimental platform was set up for a Li-ion battery pack, as shown in Fig. 5. When the controller detected that the maximum of SOC difference is beyond the limit, the equalizer began to work. On the one hand, EMB1428 was controlled through a SPI interface, and drove the switch array to work. On the other hand, the controller output three PWM signals, after being isolated and amplified by drive circuits, then drove the three MOSFETs of the single-ended forward bidirectional DC-DC converter. S1 EMB1428 S2 S3 S8 P1up P1down P2up P2down Â·Â·Â· Cell1ï¼‹ Cell1ï¼� Cell2ï¼‹ Cell7ï¼� ï¼‹ ï¼� Cell2ï¼� Cell3ï¼‹ Â·Â·Â· Equlizer Series battery pack Voltage and current acquisition and alarm processing DC-DC converter Controller Temperature acquisition and thermal management CAN communica- -tion SOC estimation Drive circuit Upper computer Switch array EMB1428 PWMSPI Fig. 4. Hardware design for the switch array Fig. 5. Battery management system experimental platform 4.2. Experimental results Equalization experiments were processed in both constant-current discharge and charge conditions. The experimental battery pack was made up of 6-series 10Ah Li[NiCoMn]O2 batteries. Once the difference between maximum and minimum SOC value was greater than 10%, the equalizer started to work, until SOC difference was less than 5%. As shown in Fig. 6, the discharge equalization experiment lasted about 32 minutes and SOC difference decreased from 12% to 5%. The charge equalization experiment lasted about 42 minutes and SOC difference dropped from 12% to 5%. Fig. 7 shows the oscilloscope waveforms during the experimental process. The output voltage in Buck mode was about 4.1V and the ripple was 7%, the output voltage in Boost mode was about 23.6V and the ripple was 6%, both of them could meet the voltage requirements for charging the single cell or the battery pack. 60 70 80 90 C el l S O C (% ) 0 10 20 30 t / min M ax S O C di ffe re nc e( % ) t / min 5 7 9 11 0 10 20 30 (1) Discharge equalization 2654 Yu Liu et al. / Energy Procedia 158 (2019) 2649â€“2654 6 Miao Gu et.al / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 0 10 20 30 40 60 70 80 90 C el l S O C (% ) t / min 0 10 20 30 40 t / min 5 7 9 11 M ax S O C di ffe re nc e( % ) (2) Charge equalization Fig. 6. SOC of 6 cells and the max difference (1) Output voltage in Buck mode (2) Output voltage in Boost mode Fig. 7. Experimental waveforms The designed equalizer can achieve energy bidirectional transmission between a single cell and battery pack. It quickly and efficiently reduces the max SOC difference to less than 5%. Besides, the structure is simple, and easy to control. 5. Conclusion Based on cell-to-pack-to-cell topology, a novel active equalizer for Li-ion battery has been designed, including a switch array and a single-ended forward bidirectional converter. The experimental results show that the designed equalizer can quickly realize battery equalization with little energy loss. This equalizer can improve the consistency of batteries, and prevent over-charge and over-discharge of a battery pack, which has good application value for the BMS in EVs. References [1] Pinto C, Barreras J V, Schaltz E, et al. Evaluation of advanced control for li-ion battery balancing systems using convex optimization[J]. IEEE Transactions on Sustainable Energy, 2016, 7(4):1703-1717. [2] Cao J, Schofield N, Emadi A. Battery balancing methods: A comprehensive review[C]// Vehicle Power and Propulsion Conference, 2008. VPPC '08, 2008:1-6. [3] Gallardo-Lozano J, Romero-Cadaval E, Milanes-Montero M I, et al. Battery equalization active methods[J]. Journal of Power Sources, 2014, 246(3):934-949. [4] Han W, Zhang L. Battery cell reconfiguration to expedite charge equalization in series-connected battery systems[J]. IEEE Robotics & Automation Letters, 2018, 3(1):22-28. [5] J.F. Reynaud, C.E. Carrejo, O. Gantet, et al. Active balancing circuit for advanced lithium-ion batteries used in photovoltaic application[C]// International Conference on Renewable Energies and Power Quality, 2010. [6] Guo Y, Lu R, Wu G, et al. A high efficiency isolated bidirectional equalizer for Lithium-ion battery string[C]// Vehicle Power and Propulsion Conference, 2012:962-966. [7] Ciortea F, Gatea C, Nemes M. High efficiency bi-directional DC-DC converter for charge balancing control of EV application-specific battery strings[C]// IEEE International Conference on Automation, Quality and Testing, Robotics, 2016:1-6. [8] Zheng W, Hu S, Niu Z, et al. Research on active equalization scheme of power battery pack[J]. Journal of Electronic Measurement and Instrumentation, 2014, 28(7):710-716. [9] Tong Y, Wu T, Jin X, et al. Study of Bi-directional DC/DC Converter[J]. Proceedings of the CSEE, 2007, 27(13):81-86. B1428 PWMSPI Fig. 4. Hardware design for the switch array Fig. 5. Battery management system experimental platform 4.2. Experimental results Equalization experiments were processed in both constant-current discharge and charge conditions. The experimental battery pack was made up of 6-series 10Ah Li[NiCoMn]O2 batteries. Once the difference between maximum and minimum SOC value was greater than 10%, the equalizer started to work, until SOC difference was less than 5%. As shown in Fig. 6, the discharge equalization experiment lasted about 32 minutes and SOC difference decreased from 12% to 5%. The charge equalization experiment lasted about 42 minutes and SOC difference dropped from 12% to 5%. Fig. 7 shows the oscilloscope waveforms during the experimental process. The output voltage in Buck mode was about 4.1V and the ripple was 7%, the output voltage in Boost mode was about 23.6V and the ripple was 6%, both of them could meet the voltage requirements for charging the single cell or the battery pack. 60 70 80 90 C el l S O C (% ) 0 10 20 30 t / min M ax S O C di ffe re nc e( % ) t / min 5 7 9 11 0 10 20 30 (1) Discharge equalization 2654 Yu Liu et al. / Energy Procedia 158 (2019) 2649â€“2654 6 Miao Gu et.al / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 0 10 20 30 40 60 70 80 90 C el l S O C (% ) t / min 0 10 20 30 40 t / min 5 7 9 11 M ax S O C di ffe re nc e( % ) (2) Charge equalization Fig. 6. SOC of 6 cells and the max difference (1) Output voltage in Buck mode (2) Output voltage in Boost mode Fig. 7. Experimental waveforms The designed equalizer can achieve energy bidirectional transmission between a single cell and battery pack. It quickly and efficiently reduces the max SOC difference to less than 5%. Besides, the structure is simple, and easy to control. 5. Conclusion Based on cell-to-pack-to-cell topology, a novel active equalizer for Li-ion battery has been designed, including a switch array and a single-ended forward bidirectional converter. The experimental results show that the designed equalizer can quickly realize battery equalization with little energy loss. This equalizer can improve the consistency of batteries, and prevent over-charge and over-discharge of a battery pack, which has good application value for the BMS in EVs. References [1] Pinto C, Barreras J V, Schaltz E, et al. Evaluation of advanced control for li-ion battery balancing systems using convex optimization[J]. IEEE Transactions on Sustainable Energy, 2016, 7(4):1703-1717. [2] Cao J, Schofield N, Emadi A. Battery balancing methods: A comprehensive review[C]// Vehicle Power and Propulsion Conference, 2008. VPPC '08, 2008:1-6. [3] Gallardo-Lozano J, Romero-Cadaval E, Milanes-Montero M I, et al. Battery equalization active methods[J]. Journal of Power Sources, 2014, 246(3):934-949. [4] Han W, Zhang L. Battery cell reconfiguration to expedite charge equalization in series-connected battery systems[J]. IEEE Robotics & Automation Letters, 2018, 3(1):22-28. [5] J.F. Reynaud, C.E. Carrejo, O. Gantet, et al. Active balancing circuit for advanced lithium-ion batteries used in photovoltaic application[C]// International Conference on Renewable Energies and Power Quality, 2010. [6] Guo Y, Lu R, Wu G, et al. A high efficiency isolated bidirectional equalizer for Lithium-ion battery string[C]// Vehicle Power and Propulsion Conference, 2012:962-966. [7] Cio

head.ce:title: A novel active equalizer for Li-ion battery pack in electric vehicles

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The school of electrical and information engineering, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300072, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The school of electrical and information engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel active equalizer for Li-ion battery pack in electric vehicles is designed. Based on cell-to-pack-to-cell topology, the equalizer consists of a switch array and a single-ended forward bidirectional DC-DC converter, which is simple, efficient and reliable. During discharging process, energy flows from the most charged cell to the battery pack, realizing peak-cut equalization. During charging process, energy transfers from the whole battery pack to the least charged cell, enabling valley-filled equalization. Using synchronous rectification, the equalizer therefore has less conduction loss. Finally, a battery management system (BMS) experimental platform for a 6-series Li-ion battery pack is set up. The experimental results show that it can quickly reduce the difference between maximum and minimum SOC value to less than 5%, which prove the feasibility and effectiveness of the designed equalizer.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Evaluation of advanced control for li-ion battery balancing systems using convex optimization[J]

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Cao J, Schofield N, Emadi A. Battery balancing methods: A comprehensive review[C]// Vehicle Power and Propulsion Conference, 2008. VPPC ‘08, 2008:1-6.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Battery cell reconfiguration to expedite charge equalization in series-connected battery systems[J]
